title: Time Series Analysis
coreConcept: Time series analysis encompasses statistical techniques applied to sequential data points collected at regular intervals. It models temporal dependencies, trends, and seasonal patterns to mathematically forecast future trajectories.
theory:
## Modeling Temporal Dynamics

Time series violate the standard independence assumption of classical regression. We must mathematically account for the fact that data at time $t$ is correlated with data at time $t-1$.

### Stationarity
A strictly stationary time series has statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation) that do not vary with time. Most analytical models require stationarity. We use differencing $\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$ to transform non-stationary data into stationary data.

### Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA)
The foundational mathematical model for forecasting. An $ARMA(p, q)$ model combines autoregression (lagged values) and moving averages (lagged errors):
$$ y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^p \phi_i y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=1}^q \theta_j \epsilon_{t-j} + \epsilon_t $$
Where $\phi_i$ are AR coefficients, $\theta_j$ are MA coefficients, and $\epsilon_t$ is white noise.

### ARIMA and Seasonal Components
By integrating the differencing step ($d$), we get the $ARIMA(p, d, q)$ model. Further extensions like SARIMA incorporate cyclic seasonal components, mathematically isolating regular periodic fluctuations.
keyTakeaways: Time series models explicitly account for the temporal autocorrelation of data points.
Stationarity is a critical mathematical prerequisite for reliable forecasting.
ARMA models blend autoregressive memory with the damping of past error shocks.
Differencing mathematically removes trends to stabilize the mean of the time series.
